cataloging_subject.label: Craft Recipes from Harley MS 2253, f. 52v
cataloging_subject.scope_note: One anonymous, manuscript-specific collection or selection of Middle English craft directions copied by the later hand conventionally called Scribe C into blank space on British Library Harley MS 2253, f. 52v. CME00137 represents nine prose recipe paragraphs in Thomas Wright's 1844 transcription: the current British Library record and the complete METS edition instead number eight manuscript articles because the gold-foil direction continues the silver-foil article. Wright's modern introduction, fourteen footnotes, two quoted Chaucer lines, and signature are edition paratext, not medieval work content.

catalog_summary.title: Craft Recipes from Harley MS 2253, f. 52v
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Composition unknown; the witness addition is dated in or soon after 1307 by Wright, ?a1350 by the MED, and by current codicological scholarship to Scribe C, probably not much later than the c.1330-40 main hand
catalog_summary.region: Unknown; the codex's West Midlands and Ludlow attribution belongs principally to the main Harley scribe and is not transferred to Scribe C or to the recipes' composition

scope.description: Relative copying chronology for the f. 52v addition

notes: The British Library explicitly excludes f. 52v from the main Harley scribe's c.1330-40 copying. Sobecki identifies the added hand as Scribe C and calls it not much later than the main hand; this is not normalized into a precise year range.

scope.description: Publication of Wright's transcription in Archaeological Journal volume 1

notes: The serial article is dated 1844; the completed bound volume's title page bears 1845. The two carrier dates remain separate in lineage.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English in Wright's introduction and notes, Latin quotations in those notes, and the two Middle English Chaucer lines in note i are edition paratext rather than additional language facets of the cataloged recipe collection.

form_statements.description: Nine Middle English procedural paragraphs in the Wright/U-M representation, corresponding to eight manuscript articles in the METS inventory. The XML's one LG and two L elements quote Chaucer inside Wright's footnote i and are excluded from the medieval collection's form.

subject_statements.label: Applying and burnishing silver and gold leaf

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English prose collection or sequence of practical craft directions preserved on Harley MS 2253, f. 52v. The procedures explain how to prepare cynople, temper azure, make three green pigments, size and burnish silver and gold foil, harden an iron edge, and manufacture white lead. The sole known witness is a later addition by the hand called Scribe C in blank space at the end of Booklet 3.

The nine Wright/U-M paragraphs cover: cynople from brazilwood; azure tempered or purified with gum water or egg white; grass-green with verdigris; another green with rotten-apple juice; gaudy-green with pennywort or gladden; silver-foil sizing and burnishing; a gold-foil variant of the same size; iron hardening with argul; and white-lead manufacture in a sealed vessel above vinegar and horse dung.

CME00137 encodes Wright's complete three-page article on Archaeological Journal printed pp.64-66: one modern English introduction paragraph, nine Middle English recipe paragraphs, fourteen lettered footnotes, three page-break markers, and Wright's signature. Its single two-line verse group is a Chaucer quotation inside footnote i and is excluded from work form and verse extent.

Nine is an edition/encoding paragraph count, while eight is the current manuscript-article count: Wright and U-M place the gold-foil continuation in its own paragraph, but METS keeps it within article 15, 'Vorte couche selverfoyl.' The preferred title and collection boundary therefore support discovery without asserting nine independently authored works or nine authorial manuscript divisions.

extent.description: Nine Middle English prose recipe paragraphs in Wright and the CME XML, all on manuscript f.52v and all represented below as content items. The current BL record and METS catalog eight manuscript articles (arts.10-17) because the gold-foil procedure is a continuation within art.15, not a separately numbered article. The encoded article has 10 P elements total (one Wright introduction plus nine recipe paragraphs), 14 NOTE elements, 3 PB elements, 2 DIV2 elements, and one LG with 2 L elements inside footnote i. Those two quoted Chaucer lines are modern editorial paratext and are excluded from intrinsic-work metrics.

content_parts.label: Wright's editorial frame and apparatus
content_parts.summary: Modern heading, article title, one-paragraph introduction, fourteen lettered footnotes, and signed closer surrounding the medieval transcription.

locators.value: DIV1/HEAD; DIV2[@TYPE='introduction']; fourteen NOTE elements in DIV2[@TYPE='recipes']; CLOSER/SIGNED

notes: The two Chaucer lines in footnote i belong exclusively to this editorial frame.

content_parts.label: Vorte make cynople
content_parts.summary: Directions for making cynople by boiling brazilwood in ditch water and pouring the reduced liquid into a cavity in chalk to cool under cover.

content_parts.label: Vorte temprene asure
content_parts.summary: Directions for tempering fine azure with gum water, suppressing bubbles with earwax, or repeatedly purifying inferior azure with prepared egg white before adding gum water.

content_parts.label: Vorte make grasgrene
content_parts.summary: Directions for making grass-green by grinding verdigris and mixing it with the finest plant juice available.

content_parts.label: Vorte maken another maner grene
content_parts.summary: Directions for another green, tempering verdigris with juice from a rotten apple.

content_parts.label: Yet for gaudegrene
content_parts.summary: Directions for gaudy-green, tempering verdigris with pennywort or gladden.

content_parts.label: Vorte couche selverfoyle
content_parts.summary: Directions for preparing a chalk, gum, and egg-white size, laying silver foil, and burnishing it with a dog's tooth.

content_parts.label: Ithe selve maner mac the sise to goldfoyl
content_parts.summary: A gold-foil variant of the preceding size, adding red ochre to strengthen its color beneath the gold.
notes: Counted as the seventh Wright/U-M procedural paragraph but not as a separate manuscript article in the METS inventory.

content_parts.label: Vorte maken iren as hart as stel
content_parts.summary: Directions for wrapping an iron tool edge in powdered argul, heating it red-hot, and quenching it in water.
notes: The one LG/two L in this XML paragraph occurs inside Wright's note i, not inside the medieval iron-hardening prose.

content_parts.label: Vorte maken blankplum
content_parts.summary: Directions for purifying and suspending thin lead strips over vinegar in a sealed vessel buried in horse dung, then collecting the resulting white lead.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00137.xml, pinned CME submodule commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD; IDG; BODY/DIV1; introduction and recipes DIV2 elements; P, NOTE, PB, LG, L, CLOSER, and SIGNED
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for identifiers, Wright's title and responsibility, one introduction paragraph, nine recipe paragraphs, fourteen footnotes, the embedded Chaucer quotation, three page breaks, and the signed closer.

Confirmed 10,231 bytes; IDG, BIBNO, and VID all CME00137; 1 DIV1, 2 DIV2, 10 P, 14 NOTE, 3 PB, 1 LG, 2 L, 2 HEAD, and 1 CLOSER/SIGNED. Nine P elements are recipes; the tenth is Wright's introduction. The LG/L elements are descendants of footnote i.

evidence.citation: Thomas Wright, 'Early English Receipts for Painting, Gilding, &c.,' Archaeological Journal 1 (1844): 64-66, in the complete Internet Archive scan of volume 1.
evidence.locator: PDF pp.82-84 = scan leaves n84-n86 = printed pp.64-66
evidence.summary: The complete 482-page bound-volume scan directly confirms Wright's article title, introduction, nine printed recipe paragraphs, fourteen lettered notes, page span, and signature, as well as the volume title page dated 1845.

evidence.citation: Archaeology Data Service Library, Archaeological Journal volume 1 issue record and Thomas Wright article detail.
evidence.summary: Official journal-index evidence for Royal Archaeological Institute publication, volume 1, year 1844, Wright's authorship, exact title, and printed page range 64-66.

evidence.summary: Official item evidence for the stable identifier, Wright article citation, displayed transcription, and item-level Public Domain / No Copyright-United States statement.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 2253 parent record 040-002048084.
evidence.locator: scope, physical characteristics, provenance, access, digitised content, and publications
evidence.summary: Current official evidence for the whole codex, including parchment, dimensions, foliation, broad late-thirteenth- to mid-fourteenth-century range, full-digital-coverage claim, current image unavailability, request restrictions, and Ker facsimile citation. The record's Ludlow origin describes the codex and main Harley context, not securely the f.52v addition.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 2253, ff.49r-142v, record 041-002352751.
evidence.locator: scope item 3; languages; date; digitised content; access
evidence.summary: Current official evidence labeling the target '8 Recipes in English (added) (f.52v)' and explicitly excluding f.52v from the Harley scribe's work. The surrounding component's c.1330-40 date and multilingual profile are not silently transferred to the added recipes.

evidence.citation: Susanna Fein, ed., with David Raybin and Jan Ziolkowski, The Complete Harley 2253 Manuscript, 3 vols. (Middle English Texts Series, 2014-2015), complete downloadable edition and current METS web edition.
evidence.locator: appendix and general introduction; combined PDF pp.516 and 556-561 = volume 2 pp.6 and 46-51; arts.10-17
evidence.summary: Current complete-manuscript scholarly edition and translation. It identifies Booklet 3, Scribe C's later use of blank f.52v, and eight English prose articles 10-17; art.15 contains both silver- and gold-foil directions, explaining the difference from nine Wright/U-M paragraphs.

evidence.citation: Sebastian Sobecki, Harley Manuscript Geographies: Literary History and the Medieval Miscellany (Manchester: Manchester University Press, 2020), open-access introduction.
evidence.summary: Modern codicological evidence that Scribe C used a column and a half of blank space on f.52v at the end of Booklet 3 for the English prose craft recipes, probably not much later than the main hand. The cached 50-page provider file is the complete open introduction, not the complete book.

evidence.citation: Middle English Dictionary, source citation '?a1350 Recipe Painting(1) in Archaeol.J.1 (Hrl 2253) 64-66,' exemplified in sinopre (n.).
evidence.locator: bibliographic source citation and quotation from printed p.64
evidence.summary: Current scholarly lexicographical evidence for the short title Recipe Painting (1), uncertain date ?a1350, Wright source location, Harley witness, and opening cynople quotation.

The linked lineage record supplies the stable source-witness, Wright-edition, U-M encoding, repository-artifact, METS-edition, Clarke-edition, and MED-catalog entities used by metadata locators and bindings.

open_questions.question: When were the recipes composed and when exactly did Scribe C copy them onto f.52v?
next_steps: Consult Ker's 1965 introduction, Clarke's 2016 edition, and current paleographical scholarship for an explicit component-level date.
Preserve Wright's in-or-soon-after-1307 estimate, the MED's ?a1350 citation, and the relative Scribe C chronology as distinct claims.
Do not substitute the c.1330-40 date of the surrounding main-scribe component for f.52v.

open_questions.question: Can Scribe C be identified or localized independently of the main Ludlow scribe?
next_steps: Consult Scribe C hand studies, the Ker facsimile introduction, LALME-related work, and current manuscript scholarship.
Keep the whole-codex Ludlow and West Midlands context out of work-level place and dialect facets until the added hand is independently localized.

open_questions.question: Do the eight manuscript articles or nine Wright/U-M paragraphs derive from one earlier recipe collection, multiple exemplars, or separately circulating procedures?
next_steps: Collate the witness and modern editions against recipe indexes and analogues.
Treat the gold-foil paragraph as a procedural unit within METS art.15 unless manuscript layout or source scholarship establishes a distinct article.
Do not create nine independently authored works from encoding paragraph boundaries alone.

open_questions.question: Can f.52v be inspected in a current first-party digital image or in person to verify layout, exact boundaries, and scribal features?
next_steps: Recheck the British Library digital image service after recovery.
Request Harley MS 2253 in London or consult Ker's 1965 facsimile under its library access conditions.
Compare direct images with Wright, METS, and Clarke before asserting diplomatic features not visible in the current public sources.

open_questions.question: Does the CME repository's software-worded root Unlicense intentionally cover the XML markup and data in CME00137?
next_steps: Seek a path-specific XML or data rights statement from the repository maintainer.
Do not broaden the repository root wording to Wright's edition, U-M's encoding, the medieval text, or manuscript images.

notes: The preferred metadata title is a supplied collection title. Wright's article title and the British Library's aggregate component label remain alternates at their own descriptive layers.

The nine-item count is reproducible for Wright's printed paragraphs and the XML P structure; the eight-article count is reproducible for the manuscript inventory. Neither count alone proves authorial organization.

Wright's fourteen footnotes include Modern English and Latin material. Footnote i also quotes two Middle English Chaucer lines; all are edition paratext and do not change the work's Middle English prose classification.

No work-level place or dialect statement is asserted. British Library Ludlow and West Midlands data describe the codex and main Harley scribe, while the same record explicitly excludes f.52v from that hand.

Rights and access for the medieval text, Wright edition, U-M encoding, repository XML, provider scans, modern editions, catalog metadata, and manuscript photography are recorded separately in lineage.
